figures.caption: Fig. 1: Illustration of a mobile service robot navigating in a private indoor environment. The privacy-sensitive object (human face) is highlighted by the red circle. Through a user-configurable multiresolution RGB perception interface, different RGB resolutions can be selected by the users according to their privacy needs. The example visualizations show how lowering resolution reduces the recognizability of privacy-sensitive content as the robot approaches the privacy object. Based on our two user studies, this figure illustrates our central design implication: privacy-preserving robot navigation can be implemented by a user-configurable distance-toresolution policy, adapting RGB resolution to both user privacy preferences and robot proximity.

Fig. 12: Comparison of subjective and objective, model-based privacy-preserving trends across RGB resolutions and robot proximity stages. The left column shows human-derived P-non curves, computed from cumulative threshold responses over valid answers (excluding N/A), while the right column shows model-based P-non values obtained from the VLM-based model. Two rows correspond to the content-privacy and recognizability dimensions. Across both human preferences and model-based evaluation, lower RGB resolutions generally yield higher P-non values, whereas closer robot proximity tends to reduce privacy preservation at a fixed resolution. Although the absolute magnitudes differ, both exhibit consistent directional trends, suggesting that privacy-aware RGB resolution preferences depend jointly on image resolution and robot proximity.

Fig. 4: Example (face) in Study 1 to assess RGB resolution thresholds for privacy preservation. The selectable options correspond to (a) 384× 384, (b) 96× 96, (c) 64× 64, (d) 32× 32, (e) 16× 16, and (f) 8× 8.

Fig. 5: Responses for the first block, which assessed users’ comfort with mobile service robots having clear visual access to private scenes and information. Specifically, (a) demonstrates participants’ attitude towards full robot vision, and (b) shows their comfort with mobile service robots capturing clear images of private imformation.

Fig. 6: Responses for the second block, which assessed user privacy perception across different image modalities. Specifically, (a) shows the image modalities participants believed could violate privacy, and (b) shows the image modalities participants believed would preserve private information.

Fig. 7: Responses for the third block, which examined users’ preferences for how mobile service robots should process privacysensitive visual data. Specifically, (a) shows participants’ comfort with mobile service robots storing clear images of private information while performing tasks, and (b) shows their preferred strategy for processing private information.
Fig. 8: Responses for the fourth block, which examined users’ perceived RGB resolution thresholds for privacy preservation under low-resolution RGB images.

Fig. 9: Overview of the adopted robot navigation pipeline. The robot’s onboard camera captures an RGB image, which is first processed by a semantic recovery module to generate a semantic segmentation map for downstream navigation. Given a target object, the system then operates in two navigation modes. If the target has not yet been detected, floor-based navigation selects the next waypoint for exploration. Once the target is found, the system switches to object-goal navigation to approach the target instance.

Fig. 11: Distribution of user-selected privacy-aware RGB resolution thresholds across navigation. Bars show valid responses (N/A excluded) for two questions: NOT contain privacy information and privacy information NOT recognizable. Response categories map to the highest acceptable RGB resolution at which the corresponding privacy-related question is considered satisfied. Lower selected resolutions therefore indicate lower acceptable RGB resolution thresholds and stronger privacy preferences. The distributions suggest that participants tended to favor lower RGB resolution thresholds as robot proximity increased.
Fig. 10: Example (private chat) in Study 2 to examine user privacy preferences for RGB resolution at near stage. The selectable options correspond to (a) 384×384, (b) 32×32, (c) 16×16, and (d) 8×8.
